title: What are Anchor Boxes?
concept: Anchor boxes are predefined reference boxes of different shapes and sizes placed at every grid cell of an image to serve as templates for detections.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main purpose of bounding box regression?

options.text: To predict adjustment offsets for draft boxes
To classify the objects in the boxes

exercises.explain: Box regression outputs offsets to fine-tune draft boxes closer to the actual objects.

cards.title: Cookie Cutters
cards.body: If you want to bake cookies shaped like stars, trees, and circles, you don't shape them by hand from scratch. You press predefined cookie cutters into the dough. In object detection, we use predefined reference shapes called anchor boxes to find objects.

cards.title: What is an Anchor Box?
cards.body: An Anchor Box (or prior box) is a predefined reference bounding box with a specific width, height, and aspect ratio. Multiple anchor boxes are centered at every grid position across the image feature map.

cards.title: The Stencil Sheet
cards.body: Imagine a plastic stencil sheet with outlines of a tall rectangle (for people), a wide rectangle (for cars), and a square (for boxes). You slide this sheet over a drawing, looking for objects that match the stencil outlines.

cards.title: YOLO's Anchor Sets
cards.body: Modern networks like YOLO define standard anchor box aspect ratios (e.g., 1:2 for standing pedestrians, 2:1 for cars, and 1:1 for square objects) to handle multiple object scales.

cards.title: Not fixed in final output
cards.body: It's a myth that anchor boxes limit detections to only those exact shapes. They are only starting drafts; the network applies regression offsets to stretch, squash, and shift them into any final shape.

cards.title: Anchor Box Varieties

exercises.prompt: What are anchor boxes in object detection?
exercises.explain: Anchor boxes are predefined reference shapes placed at each grid cell that act as templates for object proposals.

options.text: Predefined reference boxes of various shapes placed across the image grid
Weights that hold down the network layers to prevent overfitting
A type of data augmentation that flips images
The coordinates of the absolute corners of an image

exercises.prompt: True or False: Modern object detection networks use a single anchor box shape for all types of objects.
exercises.explain: False. Networks use multiple anchor boxes of different shapes and sizes to handle objects of different aspect ratios (like tall people vs. wide cars).
The network outputs regression offsets to scale and shift the anchor boxes into matching shapes.
exercises.sentence: Anchor boxes serve as starting templates, which the network modifies using ___ offsets.

exercises.prompt: Which configuration describes a standard set of anchor boxes at a grid location?
exercises.explain: Defining multiple aspect ratio dimensions at a cell represents a set of anchor boxes.

options.text: Three boxes: one tall [w=50, h=100], one wide [w=120, h=60], and one square [w=80, h=80]
Three learning rate schedules: [Cosine, Linear, Step]

exercises.prompt: You are training a model to detect trucks (long/wide objects) and trees (tall/narrow objects). At each grid cell, what anchor box configurations should you choose to improve detection accuracy?

options.text: A set of anchor boxes containing both wide aspect ratios (e.g. 3:1) for trucks and tall aspect ratios (e.g. 1:3) for trees.
A single square anchor box (1:1) to keep the calculations simple.
Only classification labels, since anchor boxes don't affect regression.
Data augmentation to rotate the trucks so they match tall boxes.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Choosing aspect ratios that match the real-world shapes of your target objects makes box regression much easier for the network. Next: let's learn how the network matches these anchors to real objects during training!
